title: The most dangerous part of a hospital discharge
summary: Everyone celebrates a successful hospital discharge. The patient is medically stable. The paperwork is complete. The prescriptions have been sent. Transportation has been arranged. Another bed becomes...
full_content:
Everyone celebrates a successful hospital discharge. The patient is medically stable. The paperwork is complete. The prescriptions have been sent. Transportation has been arranged. Another bed becomes available for the next patient who needs care. On paper, the system worked.

Yet discharge is not the finish line. It is a transition point. For hospitals, discharge often marks the successful completion of an episode of care. For patients and families, it marks the beginning of a new and often challenging phase of recovery, one that takes place outside the structured environment of the hospital and largely beyond the view of the health care team.

In reality, that is often where the real risk begins. As a community-based provider, I have learned that some of the most significant threats to a patient’s recovery do not occur inside a hospital. They occur in the days and weeks after discharge, when the safety net that existed inside the health care system suddenly disappears. The assumption is that because a patient made it home, the difficult part is over. Sometimes the difficult part is just beginning.

A patient may leave the hospital with multiple medication changes, several follow-up appointments, new equipment, and instructions that seemed clear at the time but become overwhelming once they return to everyday life. Family members and caregivers are often expected to coordinate all of it. They become transportation coordinators, medication managers, appointment schedulers, advocates, and informal care navigators almost overnight. Many are doing their best while balancing jobs, families, financial pressures, and their own health concerns. What looks manageable on a discharge checklist can quickly become unmanageable in the real world.

I have seen patients miss critical follow-up appointments because transportation fell through. I have seen medication changes misunderstood because instructions were delivered during a stressful hospital stay. I have seen caregivers become exhausted trying to manage increasingly complex care plans without ongoing support. None of these situations occurred because someone did not care. They occurred because health care often measures discharge as an event rather than a process. The discharge itself receives enormous attention. The execution afterward frequently receives much less.

The consequences are significant. Missed appointments can delay treatment. Medication errors can lead to complications. Communication breakdowns can create confusion among providers. Caregiver fatigue can affect the consistency and quality of support. Eventually, what began as a successful discharge can become an avoidable emergency department visit, readmission, or crisis.

The health care industry has spent years discussing transitions of care, yet many of our systems remain designed around handoffs rather than outcomes. We often focus on whether the discharge occurred correctly. A more important question may be whether the patient successfully navigated the first thirty days afterward. That period reveals whether the discharge plan was truly realistic. Did the patient understand the instructions? Could they obtain their medications? Did they have transportation? Did someone verify that follow-up appointments were completed? Did anyone notice when problems began to emerge?

Those questions are rarely answered by a discharge summary. They are answered through ongoing communication, coordination, and support after the patient returns home. As health care leaders continue searching for ways to improve outcomes and reduce avoidable readmissions, it may be time to shift more attention toward what happens after the celebration of discharge.

Because the most dangerous part of a hospital discharge is often the moment everyone assumes the hard part is over.

Richard Brown Jr. is a health care executive.

title: Smartphone overuse is a disease doctors ignore
summary: Imagine, for a moment, an epidemic sweeping the country. No one is immune. Its vague symptoms range from physical to mental, but they appear unrelated. Unlike during COVID, however, there is no outcry...
full_content:
Imagine, for a moment, an epidemic sweeping the country. No one is immune. Its vague symptoms range from physical to mental, but they appear unrelated. Unlike during COVID, however, there is no outcry from health officials, the government, or the public. The problem is widely felt but rarely confronted; its insidious nature allows it to pass as normal. Therefore, the medical and scientific communities have made no serious effort to diagnose or treat it. Doctors ignore it, failing to recognize the myriad ways it can present in patients and in themselves. The disease spreads unabated, wreaking havoc on society with no end in sight. What I have described is not science fiction, but reality. I am describing the disease of smartphone overuse, and primary care doctors need to start doing something about it.

Americans spend over five hours per day on their smartphones. They look at their phones while eating, waiting in line, going to the bathroom, riding in an elevator, and frighteningly, while driving. Among children, this phenomenon is particularly well documented, and serious public health efforts have been made to address it. As of late 2025, 35 states and Washington, D.C. have signed or enacted laws or policies limiting student cell phone use in K-12 classrooms. These efforts are laudable and critical, but smartphone overuse affects adults just as dramatically, and no similar public health efforts yet exist to address it. Primary care physicians can, and should, step in to fill this void.

As a primary care physician, I see patients every day who present with neck pain, blurry vision, anxiety, insomnia, inattention, and a general sense of unease. Prolonged and frequent smartphone use is often a major contributor, if not the outright cause. Repeated movement of the upper extremities in awkward postures has been shown to be a major contributing factor to musculoskeletal symptoms such as neck, shoulder, and upper back pain. Smartphone overuse is also closely associated with increased stress, decreased focus, anxiety, depression, and sleep disruptions. Despite these well-known, well-documented connections, I hardly ever discuss phone usage with my patients. Why do I, and most of my colleagues, ignore this obvious link?

I see several possible reasons. Primary care physicians are notoriously rushed and overworked. A 2022 study found that a primary care physician would require 26.7 hours per day to see an average panel of patients and follow national recommendation guidelines for preventive, chronic disease, and acute care. While AI, team-based care, and other initiatives have reduced the burden, PCPs are still forced to cover a lot, usually more than is possible, in an average visit. We’re understandably disinclined to add even more to our plates.

Moreover, for patients, discussing phone use may feel invasive and awkward. Patients come to their primary care physicians expecting guidance on things like diet and exercise, not a lecture about screen time. An element of hypocrisy may exist as well. For a PCP, counseling a patient on phone use may ring hollow when the doctor giving the advice is just as tethered to his or her device. The evidence from obesity management bears this out. A 2002 study found that patients seeking care from nonobese physicians indicated greater confidence in general health counseling and treatment of illness than patients seeing obese physicians. We primary care physicians, then, must disentangle ourselves from our phones, so we can face these challenges with our patients effectively and confidently.

But the stakes are too high to allow time constraints and timidity to impede primary care physicians from addressing this critical topic. The health consequences of phone overuse range from the predictable, psychiatric and orthopedic, to the underappreciated. Take, for example, heart disease, which is the leading cause of death in the United States. One person dies every 34 seconds from cardiovascular disease, accounting for one in every three Americans’ deaths. Smartphone overuse is a clear, new risk factor for cardiovascular disease, one which primary care physicians must get comfortable discussing with patients. Research has shown that high-frequency phone users were more likely to forgo opportunities for physical activity in order to use their phones for sedentary behaviors, and that cell phone use was significantly and negatively related to cardiorespiratory fitness.

Primary care physicians, with their broad scope of training, are perfectly positioned to diagnose and treat the condition of smartphone overuse, which affects nearly every aspect of our health. The appetite is there among patients. Two in three Americans say they want to cut back on phone use, primarily to make better use of their time; physical and mental health concerns are also driving the desire to reduce phone usage.

So how should primary care physicians broach the topic? Simply raising awareness is a start. PCPs can start by lightly probing smartphone use as part of the annual physical. Over time, this discourse should become a critical component of a patient’s social history alongside diet, exercise, sleep, and alcohol use. Ultimately, primary care physicians should examine and monitor phone use as if it were any other vital sign.

Motivational interviewing, a technique originally developed to treat addiction and substance use disorders, can be helpful. Primary care physicians have successfully utilized motivational interviewing to help patients resolve ambivalence and change their behavior. A systematic review and meta-analysis found that motivational interviewing had a significant and clinically relevant effect in approximately three out of four studies, and that physicians obtained a positive effect in approximately 80 percent of studies, including in as little as 15-minute encounters. But like many effective practices in medicine, motivational interviewing requires specialized training and time, both of which many doctors lack. Nevertheless, even a basic foundation in motivational interviewing can be helpful for PCPs to navigate and address patients’ smartphone overuse.

Motivational interviewing relies on open-ended questions, affirmations, reflections, and summary statements to empower patients and guide them toward healthier behaviors. This technique provides space for patients and doctors to acknowledge the benefits of phone use, such as staying in touch with friends and family, navigating a new city, listening to music, and booking an appointment. Motivational interviewing also recognizes that not everyone will be ready to reduce (or even discuss) their phone use, and that is OK. In fact, much like eating healthier, exercising more, or quitting smoking, physicians cannot make these changes for their patients. Our role, rather, is to meet patients with evidence-based counseling techniques and plant a seed. For some patients, and some doctors, that seed may grow into something worth putting the phone down for.

Justin Oldfield is a family physician.

title: Why the wrong physician employer can end your career
summary: Today, many physicians will be considering applying for employment at a hospital or clinic. You might be one of those. Finding your next employer is the most important decision you have made since you...
full_content:
Today, many physicians will be considering applying for employment at a hospital or clinic. You might be one of those. Finding your next employer is the most important decision you have made since you entered medical school, because selecting the wrong one may end your career as a doctor. And even if a bad employer choice does not end your career, you may have a difficult experience in re-starting your career.

A number of factors enter into this decision. The most important is finding the employer who is least likely to throw you under the bus in an adverse situation that they either manufacture or that comes from “outside” your practice. All potential employers will throw you away if necessary. That’s a given. But some, for example, in specialties that have a geographical mal-distribution, will be more “protected” than others. One key is to find a geography where you are valued. But before you consider that, you must also consider the application process itself and look for danger signals. Here are a few:

1. An extremely involved or onerous proctoring system for surgeons to qualify after probation

One OB, who had both a residency in OB and fellowships in fertility medicine, was held to 75 proctored deliveries. This doctor chose to practice in Dallas, which has an over-abundance of physicians in her specialty. If you join the OB staff in this hospital, you are not really wanted. A reasonable question could be asked: “Why did they hire her in the first place? Why didn’t she see the danger signals?”

2. Pay that is either too high or too low

If you are in a specialty that is highly desired but the pay is too high, it is unlikely that the institution will be able to continue to support you without your bringing in enormous numbers of patients. This institution will have an “unwritten policy” that you must do outreach to bring in patients for them. Unless you are prepared to do this, you may be terminated. Or at least, you will be miserable. Remember, all that is necessary for them to get rid of you is to suspend you, and in 30 days, they Data Bank you. Data Banking is the “easiest” way for any employer to get rid of you. Any “cause” can be trumped up against you. You have no constitutional rights. Your lawyer cannot “create” rights for you for which Congress has provided a carve-out in the Health Care Quality Improvement Act of 1986.

3. A history of frequent problems and “discharges” of other physicians

Some hospitals and institutions have a revolving door. If they lose many physicians year after year, you have to think that there are reasons for this. Why do you believe you will be any different from those who have gone before you?

4. How many of their employed physicians have they Data Banked?

It is very difficult to find out how many physicians got Data Banked at the institution where you want to work. But one general surgeon found out too late that Data Banking was very common in his hospital. A danger sign of this is finding out that many surgeons at the hospital lost their malpractice insurance. One of the chief reasons why physicians lose their liability insurance is from being Data Banked.

5. Are there existing lawsuits against the employer by their employees?

If ancillary employees are constantly suing their employer, you might think that the institution is “trying” to maintain quality. But a more likely explanation is that they tolerate intra-employee abuses or they do not have an adequate educational program for their employees. You may end up in the middle of major intra-employee conflicts and be totally blindsided when your employer demands that you testify against an ancillary employee.

6. Are you the only person they have in your specialty?

If you are the only employee in your specialty covering your employer’s institution, you will have to cover every exigency and emergency yourself. This means no time off. You will be responsible to take on borderline cases because you have nobody else to refer to. This situation does not occur in large hospitals, but rural institutions in disadvantaged, poor communities face this because few physicians want to practice there.

7. Are there government actions against your employer?

Any employer that is facing government actions against them may throw you into the middle of defending them, as a condition of your employment. You need to know if government actions have occurred against the employer in the past and the nature of those actions. Temporary loss of accreditation is at the top of this list of concerns. There are often explanations for such actions, but in many cases the cause is poor management. Your own professional status can become “infected” by this process. If the hospital is understaffed, or if the other employees just don’t care, their poor performance can severely damage your career.

8. Are there any whistle-blowers who have been terminated?

If you ask whistle-blowers if “it was worth it,” the majority will say it was not, even if they obtained a large settlement from the process. It is rare for there to be any financial gain for the whistle-blower at all. There is an enormous personal price to pay for making the decision to become one of this group. Most whistle-blowers have to face a very long period of time before their case becomes “resolved.” Sometimes it is five or more years as legal actions wind their way through the courts. This will utterly consume a whistle-blower’s life. Most of the time, there are good reasons for the whistle-blower to make that choice. You may be called upon by your employer to testify against them, even if you believe they were right.

9. Have any physicians under their employ in the past committed suicide?

Data Banking and sham peer reviews are used as a tool by employers and hospitals to assist in terminating physician employees. This has two benefits to them:

  • You will have less likelihood of success in a lawsuit against them.
  • You will become deeply involved in finding a way to keep practicing as a physician. As your income declines, you will not be able to finance a lawsuit against them.

Most employers will not hire a Data Banked physician. Licensing of Data Banked physicians is still possible, but it is not easy and often requires a personal appearance before a licensing board to “explain” the reasons behind such action. Data Banking is an issue that requires many pages to discuss. Suffice it to say that hospitals and institutions are now using this as a weapon against you, even if completely unwarranted. Your government is complicit in this and protects them.

Remember, if the hospital uses Data Banking as an employer’s policy, why do you think you are any different from the others?

10. Will you be called upon to testify against other physicians who have been terminated?

In the defense of a surgeon who was called to a military tribunal over his surgical history, several other employees testified against him. Even worse, those employees were “charged” with the obligation of finding “fault” with his operative choices and chart notes. The case was originally brought against him by a nurse-director of quality assurance. She was not a physician, nor did she ever observe his surgery techniques.

His surgical privileges were temporarily denied, and he left immediately. But this tribunal action followed him for five years after he left associated military employment. He was called upon to explain it in front of his licensing board. Fortunately, he prevailed. But it was by no means a certain result. Over all, it cost him nearly $100,000 to repair.

Summary

If you believe that your problems associated with your now private practice will end when you become employed, think again. Your selection of your employer is the most important decision you can make and requires enormous investigative efforts. The resources of your employer dwarf yours. Yet you need to learn about them in detail.

Ultra-high-risk employers

  • Employment contracts that are unfair, are unreasonable, have non-competes, or have red flags.
  • Large hospital chains.
  • Critical access hospitals, rural hospitals, and hospitals with a poor payer mix.
  • Any facility that offers income guarantees or low-interest personal loans.
  • Any facility that refuses to permit residents or interns.
  • Native American health care clinics and hospitals hire under probation. They will just Data Bank anyone they want to get rid of.
  • If you get hired by a group practice, who is fronting the funds? If the hospital is fronting the money, they have a much greater likelihood of Data Banking you as a tactic.

Some years ago, physicians wanted to find access to information about hospitals who Data Banked physicians at a higher rate, as a registry. So far, this has not been enacted by any state legislature. The contract you obtain from your new employer is nebulous and means virtually nothing, because if you sue them, you will find it almost impossible to obtain another hospital position.

There are no secrets. People talk.

Richard A. Lawhern is a nationally recognized health care educator and patient advocate who has spent nearly three decades researching pain management and addiction policy. His extensive body of work, including over 300 published papers and interviews, reflects a deep critique of U.S. health care agencies and their approaches to chronic pain treatment. Now retired from formal academic and hospital affiliations, Richard continues to engage with professional and public audiences through platforms such as LinkedIn, Facebook, and his contributions to KevinMD. His advocacy extends to online communities like Protect People in Pain, where he works to elevate the voices of patients navigating restrictive opioid policies. Among his many publications is a guideline on opioid use for chronic non-cancer pain, reflecting his commitment to evidence-based reform in pain medicine.

Richard Willner is a health care executive.

title: The quiet skills that actually save a medical career
summary: Medicine trains us to master physiology, pharmacology, and diagnostic reasoning. We spend years learning how to manage ventilators, interpret imaging, and navigate complex clinical decisions. But the ...
full_content:
Medicine trains us to master physiology, pharmacology, and diagnostic reasoning. We spend years learning how to manage ventilators, interpret imaging, and navigate complex clinical decisions. But the skills that actually determine whether a physician survives a career, or is broken by it, are almost never taught. They are quiet skills. Uncelebrated skills. Skills that determine whether you can practice with dignity in a system that often makes dignity difficult.

After four decades in medicine, across clinical practice, academic leadership, and institutional transitions, I have learned that the physicians who endure are not the ones with the highest test scores or the most publications. They are the ones who master four competencies that rarely appear in any curriculum.

Maintaining dignity under institutional pressure

Every physician eventually encounters a moment when the institution’s priorities diverge from the patient’s needs, or from the physician’s own moral center. It may be subtle: a pressured discharge, a productivity demand, a documentation requirement that distorts the truth. Or it may be overt: retaliation for speaking up, pressure to conform, or the quiet expectation to look away.

The physicians who survive are not the ones who fight every battle. They are the ones who know which battles matter, and who can hold their dignity without burning themselves to ash. Dignity is not defiance. Dignity is steadiness. It is the ability to say, “This is the line I will not cross,” and to hold that line without theatrics, self-destruction, or apology.

Navigating collapsing systems without losing your moral center

Every generation of physicians believes the system is collapsing, but today’s clinicians face pressures that are qualitatively different: corporatization, loss of autonomy, moral injury, the erosion of continuity, and the replacement of judgment with metrics.

The quiet skill here is not cynicism. It is moral endurance. It is the ability to practice medicine inside a system that often undermines the very values that brought you into the profession, without becoming numb, bitter, or complicit. This requires a kind of internal architecture: a clear sense of who you are, what you stand for, and what you will not allow the system to take from you.

Physicians who lack this architecture drift. Physicians who build it survive.

Mentoring without creating dependency

Medicine romanticizes mentorship, but the truth is more complicated. A mentor who creates dependency is not a mentor. They are a gravitational field. They pull trainees into their orbit, limit their growth, and confuse loyalty with development.

The quiet skill is teaching people in a way that allows them to leave you. A good mentor does the following:

  • Gives judgment, not just information
  • Models steadiness, not perfection
  • Teaches the craft, not the performance
  • Prepares the trainee to stand alone

The best mentors are the ones whose students succeed without needing them. That is not loss. That is legacy.

Exiting a role with grace and continuity

No one teaches physicians how to leave, how to step out of a role, a department, or a system without chaos, resentment, or unfinished business. Yet exits shape reputations more than entrances ever do.

A graceful exit requires:

  • Clarity
  • Preparation
  • The discipline to hand off responsibilities cleanly
  • The humility to let others take ownership
  • The foresight to leave the institution stronger than you found it

The physicians who exit well are the ones who understand that continuity is a form of care, not just for patients, but for colleagues, trainees, and the institution itself. Leaving well is not retreat. It is stewardship.

Why these skills matter now

Mid-career physicians feel the ground shifting beneath them. They see the pressures rising, the metrics tightening, the autonomy shrinking, and the culture changing. Many feel unprepared, not clinically, but existentially.

The quiet skills are what carry you through these moments. They are what allow you to practice medicine with integrity in a system that often rewards the opposite. These skills do not appear on board exams. They do not earn RVUs. They do not show up in promotion criteria. But they are the skills that determine whether a physician can sustain a career without losing the parts of themselves that matter.

The truth we rarely say aloud

Medicine will always demand intelligence, stamina, and technical competence. But the physicians who endure, the ones who remain whole, are the ones who cultivate the quiet skills. They are the ones who understand that survival in medicine is not about brilliance. It is about character. And character is built not in the dramatic moments, but in the quiet ones, the moments when no one is watching, when the institution is indifferent, and when the only person who knows whether you upheld your values is you.

Those are the skills that save a medical career. And they are the skills worth teaching.

Ronald L. Lindsay is a retired developmental-behavioral pediatrician whose career spanned military medicine, academic leadership, and national advocacy for dignity-centered neurodevelopmental care. His NIH-funded work with the RUPP Autism Network helped define evidence-based approaches to autism and related developmental disorders.

He directed the LEND Program at The Ohio State University and founded JBLM CARES, a $10 million autism resource center for military families. His writing spans clinical scholarship and long-form fiction. He is the author of The Mercy Directive and the six-novel Cassandra series, a completed political and medical fiction saga tracing the rise of the Cassandra system from its origins to its national and international legacy. His forthcoming memoir, The Quiet Architect, examines how conscience and structure collide in modern medicine.

He shares updates on LinkedIn.

title: 全球首个双抗ADC宜泽康®京东DTP药房全国首发
summary: 全球首个获批上市的EGFR×HER3双抗ADC药物宜泽康®在京东DTP药房全国首发，中国复发\u002F转移性鼻咽癌患者将率先享有“双抗+ADC”这一前沿技术方案。鼻咽癌在中国南方及东南亚高发，复发\u002F转移性患者后线治疗选择有限，该药为临床提供全新突破性方案。作为全球首创双抗ADC，宜泽康®打破传统路径，实现更精准肿瘤识别，提升杀伤效率并降低脱靶毒性，标志肿瘤治疗迈入精准新时代。此次宜泽康...
full_content:
近日，全球首个获批上市的EGFR×HER3双抗ADC药物伦康依隆妥单抗（商品名：宜泽康®）正式在京东DTP药房全国首发开售。

 


这不仅是该创新药自今年6月获国家药品监督管理局批准后的最快落地行动，更意味着中国复发/转移性鼻咽癌患者将率先享有“双抗+ADC”这一全球前沿技术方案，而京东健康则在这一进程中扮演了打通“临床可及最后一公里”的关键角色。

 

鼻咽癌是一类具有区域高发特征的头颈部恶性肿瘤，在中国南方及东南亚地区高发，复发/转移性患者一旦对标准系统化疗和PD-1/PD-L1抑制剂均产生耐药或进展，后线治疗选择便极为有限，疾病控制与长期生存面临严峻挑战。该药用于既往经至少二线系统化疗和免疫治疗失败的成人患者，为临床提供了机制全新的突破性方案。

 

作为百利天恒自主研发的全球首创双抗ADC，宜泽康®打破了传统单靶点或单抗ADC的固有路径。其通过同时靶向EGFR和HER3两个异源性肿瘤相关抗原，实现更精准的肿瘤识别，并高效递送细胞毒性载荷，显著提升杀伤效率的同时降低脱靶毒性。这一“双抗+ADC”的复合策略，首次以正式获批药物的形态走向临床，标志着肿瘤治疗从“单靶点干预”迈入“多靶点协同”的精准新时代，也彰显了中国创新药在全球前沿赛道上的领跑实力。

 

创新药的价值，最终取决于患者能否及时、规范地用上药。此次宜泽康®上市即同步覆盖京东DTP药房全国12个核心城市，并全面启动处方药专业服务流程：从处方审核、药品配送、用药指导到药师持续答疑，京东DTP药房以标准化、可追溯的全链条服务，确保每一位患者都能获得与院内同质化的用药保障。

 

京东健康相关负责人表示：“我们不仅要做创新药的‘首发渠道’，更要做患者全病程管理的‘专业伙伴’。借助京东健康的数智化供应链和覆盖全国的DTP专业药房网络，我们能够将宜泽康®从获批到临床应用的周期大幅缩短，同时依托在线医师、药师团队，为患者提供7×24小时用药支持，真正让前沿疗法更快的触达到每一位患者。”

 

宜泽康®在京东DTP药房的首发，并非单点事件，而是京东健康深度参与创新药商业化闭环的又一范例。凭借强大的处方承接能力、冷链配送体系及专业药事服务能力，京东DTP药房已成长为众多全球首创药物国内落地的首选合作终端。未来，京东健康将继续携手百利天恒等头部创新药企，加速更多突破性疗法的可及化进程，让中国患者不仅“用得上新药”，更能“用得对、用得安心”。

 

从全球首创到全国可及，从精准靶点到专业服务，百利天恒与京东DTP药房的此次联手，既为复发/转移性鼻咽癌患者提供了更多元的治疗选择，也为中国创新药价值的快速普及提供了助力。

title: 国产神外手术机器人迎来“高光时刻”，第一股要来了！
summary: 中国神经外科手术领域正经历变革，6月30日华科精准科创板IPO招股书获受理，有望成国产神外手术机器人第一股。传统神经外科手术中，从头架固定到头部钉骨钉，给患者带来极大痛苦，且病灶定位、手术路径规划与实施若不精准会带来不可逆伤害。中国癫痫等神经系统疾病患者规模大，诊疗需求持续增长，临床痛点广泛且深刻。2015年刘文博等人创立华科精准，投入研发。2018年其首款神经外科手术机器人SR1获批上市，是国内...
full_content:
从头架固定、头部钉骨钉的“上刑”般痛苦场景，到30秒无创伤、精准注册，中国神经外科手术领域正在经历一场变革。


6月30日，华科精准科创板IPO招股书获受理，这份文件清晰地呈现出上述变革的技术路径与产业逻辑。


这家聚焦于神经外科精准手术医疗器械的企业，也已凭借全球首创的3D结构光注册技术攻克临床痛点，拿下国内神经外科手术机器人53.3%市场份额，甚至收获了来自竞争对手、神外领域国际巨头美敦力的投资，有望成为国产神外手术机器人第一股。



神经外科手术机器人究竟带来了怎样的改变？这要从一个典型的传统手术场景说起。


华科精准联合创始人、首席技术官刘文博从清华大学生物医学工程学院博士毕业后，一直从事手术机器人和手术导航方面的研究和成果转化，一次临床观摩给他带来了极大触动。


那是一场癫痫手术，患者是一个十一二岁的小女孩。医生需要给患者上头架，用4颗钉子固定到头骨上，操作还必须在患者清醒状态下进行，无法进行全麻。小女孩疼到表情扭曲，全程不断哭泣。


在大多数医生和患者的认知中，为治疗疾病而忍受必要的检查或手术痛苦，是一种无奈之下的理所当然。不过，在刘文博看来，得病已经让病人很痛苦，传统手术方式无异于是对病人的二次伤害，一定有更好的办法解决这样的问题。


的确，临床痛点广泛且深刻。


癫痫是全球最常见的慢性脑部疾病之一，中国癫痫患者规模较大，总体患病率约为0.7%，活动性癫痫患病率约为0.46%。癫痫在所有年龄段均可发生，其中，儿童与老年人群常呈现更高的风险和更复杂的病因构成。随着人口老龄化加剧，脑肿瘤、脑出血、帕金森病等其他神经系统疾病的诊疗需求也在持续增长。


这些庞大群体在接受外科手术时，病灶定位、手术路径的规划与实施必须足够精准，否则会带来不可逆的伤害。而传统手术方式本身，就已构成对身体和心理的双重伤害。


2015年，刘文博与另外两名创始人共同创立了华科精准，投入神经外科手术机器⼈等一系列精准手术设备研发。2018年，华科精准首款神经外科手术机器人SR1获批上市，是国内首款通过国家创新医疗器械特别审批并成功上市的神经外科手术机器人。


SR1系列的高端型号、搭载了3D结构光注册技术的SR1-3D于2020年获NMPA批准上市，又于2023年获FDA认证，成为国内唯一同时获得FDA与NMPA双重认证的神经外科手术机器人。


 传统头架（左）和神经外科手术机器人（右），图片来源：招股书


神经外科手术机器人带来的关键变化在于：取代传统头架，推动神经外科微创术式革新。曾经需要在清醒状态下忍受“刑具”一样的头架固定之痛的患者，如今可以在无标记物、无额外CT扫描的条件下完成手术定位。


凭借临床价值，华科精准的产品已获得市场高度认可。在中国神经外科智能化导航定位设备领域，2025年，华科精准以33.6%的市场份额位居第一；在其中的神经外科手术机器人细分领域，则以53.3%的市场份额排名第一。



华科精准之所以能在短短几年时间里占据领先地位，关键在于其核心产品的3D结构光注册技术。


3D结构光注册技术是一种基于计算机视觉的高精度、快速无接触式注册方式。依托该技术，SR1系列手术机器人可实现3D结构光增强现实投影功能，将病灶组织、关键血管、神经纤维束等重要解剖结构，直接可视化投射于患者头皮表层，帮助医生直观开展手术切口规划，精准定位病灶并完成精细化切除，简化术中操作流程，大幅提升手术切口设计的合理性与术中操作精准度，切实优化临床手术效果。


 

SR1系列手术机器人搭载3D结构光注册技术，图片来源：华科精准官网


与传统标记物注册相比，该技术可实现免标记物注册，仅需30秒即可完成，注册速度提高 5-10倍，且无需加扫术前定位CT，能减少患者痛苦和可能的创伤；还可实现整头扫描与颅骨投影，且不受体位限制，可获取百万量级高密度点云，比激光注册高300倍，注册精度平均达到0.5mm。


除了最核心的SR1系列手术机器人之外，华科精准还围绕其他临床细分痛点，搭建起多元化的神经外科智能导航定位设备体系。


其中，Q300系列神经外科微型手术机器人主打轻量化、小型化，在紧凑空间内集成四自由度高精度机器人平台，可在微型化机身尺寸下，兼顾传统导航系统与大型手术机器人的核心功能，尤其适配基层医疗机构。


随着神经外科病种日趋复杂，神经脊柱外科作为神经外科的一个分支快速发展。在这一趋势下，华科精准推出X1000系列脑与脊柱手术机器人，覆盖神经外科及脊柱外科两大科室需求，可开展脑深部电极置入术、脊柱椎弓根钉内固定术等十余种术式。产品采用了模块化系统框架，灵活完成软硬件功能模块集成与扩展，适配医疗机构的个性化需求。


面向开放式手术，华科精准则有NS1系列智能导航系统，帮助医生在复杂解剖结构中规避功能区风险，实现眼手协同的标准化操作。


在持续进行技术创新的同时，高度协同的全产业链产品矩阵是华科精准另一大核心竞争力。


除了神经外科智能化导航定位设备之外，华科精准的颅内电极也是核心产品，并已占国内81.3%的市场份额，稳居第一；磁共振引导激光消融治疗系统LS1则是国内首款获批的该类型设备，填补了国内市场空白。


目前，华科精准在神经外科领域已有12款三类医疗器械获批，产品累计覆盖全国所有省级行政区，服务超400家医院。


 华科精准产品矩阵构建的整体协同解决方案，图片来源：招股书


依托产品布局，华科精准已形成设备与软件之间、设备与耗材之间功能互补、数据互通的模式，既能更完整地解决医患真实痛点，也形成了具有独特壁垒、可持续的商业模式。2023年到2025年，华科精准年收入已从1.48亿元增长至1.89亿元。



值得一提的是，华科精准竟获得了来自竞争对手的投资。招股书信息显示，国际医疗器械巨头美敦力通过创投基金对华科精准持股3.12%。


在神经外科精准手术医疗器械领域，市场主要由美敦力等国际医疗科技巨头主导，这些大公司依托其全球化经营的优势，长期保持着雄厚的研发投入和技术储备能力。在国内神外智能导航系统这一细分市场，美敦力至今仍占据44.5%的份额（2025年数据），位居榜首。


拆解双方的产品布局不难发现，美敦力的投资逻辑大概是：与其被动应对国产冲击，不如主动寻求与潜在挑战者的合作。


具体来看，在神经外科智能化导航定位设备领域（即导航+手术机器人），华科精准仅有一款导航设备，手术机器人则有三款，可覆盖大三甲到基层的各类医疗机构。而美敦力在中国市场则以导航设备为主。同时，国产设备更贴合国内医生操作习惯、适配国内医院成本预算，且能提供更高效的本地化技术服务。


在此背景下，美敦力避开全线正面竞争，转而投资本土头部优秀企业，这既是对华科精准的认可，也可视为采取错位竞争、平衡市场格局的策略。


当前，华科精准正在新一代磁共振引导激光消融治疗系统的研发，依托定向消融技术攻克颅内肿瘤治疗的核心技术难关。同时围绕神经调控、脑机接口等脑科学前沿方向，推进脑卒中康复迷走神经刺激系统、新型脑柔性电极、脑机接口植入式手术机器人等在研管线。本次IPO拟募资近10亿元，其中近半数投向神经外科医疗器械研发项目。


 华科精准产品管线，图片来源：招股书


不难看出，华科精准的一大重点方向是推进高端手术器械与脑机接口的融合。长远来看，未来创新产品的价值远不止于消除手术带来的身心创伤，更在于加速脑科学前沿技术落地，带来更深层次的临床获益。

title: 扬子江药业集团男科创新药盐酸妥诺达非片（泰妥妥®）全面上市
summary: 近日，扬子江药业集团宣布旗下男科创新药盐酸妥诺达非片（泰妥妥®）全面上市。该药是自主研发的5型磷酸二酯酶（PDE5）抑制剂1类创新药，用于治疗勃起功能障碍（ED）。通过分子结构优化设计，提高了对靶点的抑制活性及选择性，在保证良好疗效的同时，受食物代谢和饮酒影响小，用药更灵活。Ⅲ期临床研究显示，该药可改善ED患者勃起功能，相关研究结果已于2026年在世界性医学大会（WMSM 2026）上进行口头报告...
full_content: 近日，扬子江药业集团宣布，旗下男科创新药盐酸妥诺达非片（泰妥妥®）全面上市。盐酸妥诺达非片是由扬子江自主研发的5型磷酸二酯酶（PDE5）抑制剂1类创新药，用于治疗勃起功能障碍（ED)1。其通过分子结构优化设计，提高了对靶点的抑制活性及选择性，在保证良好临床疗效的同时，受食物代谢和饮酒影响小，用药更加灵活。Ⅲ期临床研究显示，盐酸妥诺达非片可改善ED患者勃起功能。相关研究结果已于2026年在世界性医学大会（WMSM 2026）上进行口头报告。

title: 【首发】瀚越高新材料完成数千万元 Pre-A 轮融资，深耕一次性解决方案赛道
summary: 2026年7月3日，瀚越高新材料（含旗下瀚越生物材料上海有限公司）正式宣布完成数千万元人民币Pre-A轮融资，本轮融资由龙磐投资独家出资，羿水澜山作为独家顾问，提供财务融资、业务规划等战略支持！本轮Pre-A轮融资，将彻底补齐我们的产能短板，加速专业化、合规化、规模化生物制药一次性产线落地，全面提升批量交付与快速定制服务能力。\n\n...
full_content:
近日，瀚越高新材料（含旗下瀚越生物材料上海有限公司）正式宣布完成数千万元人民币Pre-A轮融资，本轮融资由龙磐投资独家出资，羿水澜山作为独家顾问，提供财务融资、业务规划等战略支持！




作为聚焦高端新材料与生物制药核心配套领域的优质企业，瀚越高新材料以及其集团公司深耕行业多年，精准布局高景气的生物制药一次性解决方案赛道，依托自主可控的材料研发技术、稳定合规的产品品质及一站式配套服务体系，在细分领域构筑起深厚的技术与市场壁垒。


伴随全球生物药、细胞与基因治疗、mRNA疫苗等生物医药产业高速扩容，无菌、高效、安全的一次性流体解决方案已成为生物制药生产工艺升级的核心刚需，替代传统不锈钢设备的行业趋势明确，叠加国内生物医药装备国产化替代政策红利，行业迎来持续稳健的增长周期。




公司核心业务聚焦高端工业新材料、生物制药一次性流体传输零部件等关键领域，旗下瀚越生物材料（上海）有限公司深耕生物制药专用材料的研发、量产与场景化落地，包括：


● 低溶析出，高精度的硅胶管路

● 可焊接，可热封的热塑管路

● 其它各种生物工艺流体传输，灌装的管路和配件等


公司主打适配生物制药无菌生产、密闭流体传输场景的一次性配套解决方案，可有效解决药企生产过程中的交叉污染风险、降低清洗验证成本、提升工艺迭代与量产效率，完美契合生物医药产业绿色低碳、高效合规、柔性生产的发展趋势，为客户提供一站式解决方案。




凭借材料具有极好的生物相容性、高稳定性、低溶析出的核心优势及定制化能力，公司产品广泛适配各类生物制药生产场景，精准匹配行业高标准合规要求，累计收获众多生物医药领域头部客户的长期认可与深度合作，持续抢占国内生物制药一次性材料国产化替代的核心市场机遇。




除生物制药赛道外，公司依托成熟的新材料智造技术体系，同步布局工业制造、通用医疗、半导体、精细化工等多元领域流体传输管路产品矩阵，针对不同行业严苛工况打造高性能专用管线系列，全面覆盖复杂流体输送场景。该类管路产品在耐高温、耐高压、长蠕动泵寿命、耐酸碱及各类化学品腐蚀等方面表现突出，可适配多行业极端工况使用需求。



主打凯迈斯双层蠕动泵专用输送管等工业级管路，采用共挤复合结构与无增塑剂惰性内孔设计，内层可抵御各类水性流体、洗涤剂、工业试剂吸附侵蚀，外层强化耐弯折性能，大幅延长蠕动泵连续运行寿命，具备优异耐高压能力，可满足化工分配、商用清洁、大型基建流体泵送等场景需求，有效适配工业连续化生产工况。


同时推出氟塑料系列工业管路，拥有极强化学稳定性，可长期耐受强酸、强碱、有机溶剂等强腐蚀介质，适配精细化工、电镀、表面处理等高腐蚀流体输送环节，宽温域性能稳定，可应对生产过程中的温度波动与介质冲击。



延伸布局非药类医疗配套管路，延续低析出、高生物相容的核心特性，同时强化耐蒸汽高温消毒、反复弯折、耐医用清洗剂腐蚀能力，适配医疗设备流体输送、实验室试剂传输、消毒介质流转等场景，兼顾洁净度与耐用性，符合医疗行业基础安全标准。



推出超高纯特种流体管路，采用高纯氟系材料打造，具备极低溶出、低离子析出、高洁净度特性，满足半导体超纯水、光刻胶、电子级酸碱蚀刻液、特种工艺气体的输送要求，可严格管控微量杂质污染，契合芯片制造纳米级生产标准。


管路支持高温工况作业，同时凭借优异耐压性能保障密闭输送，内壁光滑无滞留，可规避高纯介质交叉污染问题，适配晶圆制造、芯片封装等全流程高纯流体传输环节。



本轮Pre-A轮融资的顺利落地，是资本市场对瀚越高新材料在生物制药一次性解决方案细分赛道技术壁垒、产品合规能力与国产替代成长潜力的高度认可。


瀚越高新材料董事长王连祥表示：非常感谢龙磐投资的深度认可与战略加持，也感谢羿水澜山等产业伙伴对公司发展的关注与支持。


当前国内生物制药行业已从增量扩张进入提质增效、国产替代的关键周期，一次性流体传输解决方案作为生物制药生产的核心刚需，贯穿配液、传输、灌装、取样全工艺过程，是保障药品安全、降低污染风险、缩短验证周期、适配柔性生产的关键核心配套。

随着下游创新药、CDMO、疫苗企业产能持续扩张，市场对高合规、低溶析出、高稳定性的国产一次性耗材及专用基材料需求爆发式增长。过去一段时间，公司凭借过硬的产品合规性与场景化解决方案，拿下众多药企验证落地，但受限于产能规模，无法充分承接快速增长的国产替代订单。


本轮Pre-A轮融资，将彻底补齐我们的产能短板，加速专业化、合规化、规模化生物制药一次性产线落地，全面提升批量交付与快速定制服务能力。


王连祥进一步表示，未来公司将聚焦生物制药一次性解决方案主赛道，将融资金精准投入核心材料迭代、精密零部件产线扩容、洁净生产体系升级与合规验证体系完善。


公司将持续深耕生物制药流体传输全场景，持续优化一次性管路、接头、传输组件及专用基材产品体系，打造“材料研发 - 工艺优化 - 合规验证 - 规模化交付”的一站式国产解决方案。


依托规模化产能优势，进一步压缩交付周期、优化成本结构，助力下游药企降本增效、实现供应链自主可控，持续深耕创新药、CGT、疫苗、生物制剂等高增长细分场景，持续抢占生物制药一次性耗材国产化替代的核心红利，持续扩大行业市场占有率。


与此同时，公司将同步加码新材料智造板块，加大工业、医疗、半导体、精细化工等跨领域高性能流体管路的技术研发与产线扩产，持续优化管路耐高温、耐高压、耐化学腐蚀、长寿命等核心性能，丰富多行业定制化流体传输产品矩阵，打通生物制药与高端制造双赛道协同发展路径，拓宽国产高端流体材料的应用边界。


龙磐投资项目负责人表示：国内生物制药产业正迎来结构性升级机遇，一次性使用系统（SUS）全面替代传统不锈钢生产体系已成行业不可逆趋势。


伴随单抗、双抗、细胞与基因治疗、疫苗、CGT等创新药赛道高速爆发，叠加供应链安全自主可控的政策导向，生物制药一次性流体传输耗材、核心基材料的国产替代空间巨大，行业连续多年保持20%以上增速，是确定性极强的高景气赛道。当前国内高端生物制药一次性核心材料与精密零部件仍高度依赖进口，行业存在明显的技术缺口与供给缺口。


瀚越高新材料深耕生物制药一次性配套领域多年，聚焦流体传输核心场景，实现了材料配方、低析出工艺、生物相容合规、规模化量产的全链条自主可控，产品可直接对标进口体系，有效解决药企成本高、交期长、供应链不稳定的痛点。


团队产业化经验扎实、产品落地验证充分，客户口碑突出，我们坚定看好公司在生物制药一次性国产化浪潮中的核心价值，本轮融资将助力公司快速释放产能、抢占进口替代市场份额，夯实细分赛道龙头地位。


作为长期深耕生物医药产业供应链服务的专业机构，羿水澜山总裁朱凤山对瀚越高新材料本轮融资及行业发展趋势发表专业评论：生物制药一次性解决方案是支撑创新药、CGT、疫苗等高端领域产业化的关键基础设施，当前国内正处于从依赖进口转向自主可控的关键拐点。瀚越高新材料在材料配方、低溶析出工艺、生物相容性验证、规模化量产等环节实现全链条自主突破，产品直接对标国际一线品牌，精准解决了国内药企长期面临的成本高、交期长、供应链不稳等核心痛点。


此次Pre-A轮融资落地，将有效补齐公司产能短板，加速合规化、规模化产线落地，进一步夯实其在生物制药一次性流体传输耗材赛道的领先优势。羿水澜山持续看好生物制药核心耗材国产化赛道，未来也将依托产业资源与专业服务能力，助力瀚越高新材料这类具备核心技术壁垒的本土企业加速成长，共同推动生物医药产业链供应链自主可控进程。


业内分析指出，国内生物制药一次性技术赛道正处于黄金增长期，行业规模持续扩容，进口替代空间广阔。相较于传统不锈钢生产系统，一次性解决方案具备无交叉污染、无需清洗验证、投产快、适配柔性生产、低碳节能等核心优势，完美契合创新药产业化发展需求，已成为生物医药产业升级的核心抓手。


此前高端一次性生物耗材与核心材料长期被海外品牌垄断，供应链安全性、成本可控性、交付时效性均存在短板。随着瀚越高新材料等本土优质企业完成技术突破与产能升级，国产替代进入加速落地阶段。


本轮资本加持后，瀚越高新材料将实现产能、技术、合规、交付能力的全方位升级，进一步填补国内高端生物制药一次性流体传输解决方案的供给缺口，助力国内生物医药产业链、供应链自主可控，加速推动行业高端材料与核心零部件的国产化进程。


而其跨领域布局的高性能工业、半导体、通用医疗管路产品，也将依托同源材料技术优势，打破海外高端流体管路在多领域的垄断局面，为国内高端制造产业链供应链自主可控补充关键配套力量。



瀚越高新材料是国内专注于生物制药一次性技术解决方案与高端配套材料的高新技术企业，旗下瀚越高新材料(常州)有限公司以及瀚越生物材料(上海)有限公司为核心研发与产业化主体，聚焦生物制药专用基材料、一次性流体传输核心零部件、整套一次性工艺配套解决方案的研发、合规生产与市场化落地。公司深耕生物制药无菌生产、密闭流体传输、工艺取样、药液输送等核心场景，针对生物药、疫苗、CGT、CDMO等高端生产需求，打造高生物相容性、低析出、高耐候性的系列产品，全面适配药企GMP合规生产标准，可完整替代进口一次性配套系统。公司以解决行业“卡脖子”材料与零部件短板、实现供应链自主可控为核心目标，依托自主研发的材料配方与精密生产工艺，为生物医药企业提供高性价比、高稳定性、快交付的一站式国产一次性解决方案，深度赋能国内生物医药产业升级与国产化替代。公司同时深耕新材料智造领域，面向工业、通用医疗、半导体、精细化工等行业，研发生产耐高低温、耐高压、长寿命、强耐化学腐蚀的全系列流体传输管路及配件，可满足多行业复杂工况下的流体输送定制化需求，构建多领域协同发展的高端新材料产业布局。



龙磐投资总部位于北京，是中国领先的生物医药风险投资机构。目前管理5只人民币基金与1只美元基金，总规模超过人民币100亿元，是迄今唯一同时获得全国社保基金和国家中小企业发展基金出资支持的生物医药投资机构。龙磐投资专注于创新药与创新医疗器械领域，主要投资早中期项目，累计投资企业超150家，其中创新药企业130余家、创新器械企业20余家。代表性企业包括：贝达药业（SZ.300558）、荣昌生物（SH.688331；HK.09995）、爱博诺德（SH.688050）、亚虹医药（SH.688176）、凯因科技（SH.688687）、宜明昂科（HK.01541）、康蒂尼药业（NASDAQ:GYRE）、华昊中天（HK.02563）、维立志博（HK.09887）等众多明星项目。龙磐投资团队多位合伙人拥有20年以上的国际/国内生物医药产业研发与管理经验，致力于为被投企业提供系统化、全方位的战略增值支持。



羿水澜山（简称羿山资本）于2022年8月诞生在华夏经济金融之都-上海（陆家嘴），是专注于生物医药产业投融资与产业资源整合的专业服务机构，长期深耕生物制药高端耗材、核心材料、工艺装备等国产化赛道，为具备技术壁垒与成长潜力的硬科技企业提供投融资顾问、产业对接、合规赋能等一站式服务，助力本土企业突破进口垄断、加速产业化落地，推动生物医药产业链自主可控与高质量发展。

title: Can emotional regulation in medicine reduce errors?
summary: Health care professionals are trained to diagnose, decide, and act under pressure. Yet one of the most important factors influencing clinical judgment, patient trust, and resilience is often overlooke...
full_content:
Health care professionals are trained to diagnose, decide, and act under pressure. Yet one of the most important factors influencing clinical judgment, patient trust, and resilience is often overlooked: emotional self-regulation.

Emotional regulation is not just a personal wellness practice that clinicians might pursue outside work to reduce stress or improve work-life balance. Today, it is increasingly being recognized as an essential clinical competency to practice, to reset, and to renew our energy during the workday.

When we get overwhelmed or overloaded, our emotions and nervous system can become dysregulated. When we’re out of sync, it limits our clarity and intuitive discernment. In fast-paced clinical environments, this has direct implications for patient care, communication, teamwork, and decision-making. We don’t have access to our best self.

The physiology of a stressed nervous system

Chronic stress and emotional overload desynchronize the autonomic nervous system, which controls 90 percent of the body’s involuntary functions, including hormonal and immune system balance, digestion, elimination, and more. The body becomes out of balance. Our heart rate variability (HRV) or heart rhythm pattern reflects the balance in our autonomic nervous system. This pattern signals the brain through the vagal nerve when the body is under stress, and the brain responds with a fight, flight, or fright (anxiety) stress response.

This stress response evolved to help humans move into survival mode to escape immediate threats. Today, however, many people, including health professionals, chronically experience this stress response. When stress becomes chronic, it creates elevated cortisol, sleep disruption, and other physical symptoms. On a mental level, our higher brain functions (executive decision-making, intuitive discernment, and clear reasoning) become less available.

Clinicians know these effects well because they experience them. Many feel trapped in systems that require them to move from patient to patient every 15 or 20 minutes while managing growing administrative burdens. Over time, exhaustion and compassion fatigue can drain the inspiration that originally brought many to health care.

When clinicians are emotionally dysregulated, their listening to patients can become impaired. They may say the right words but are internally processing stress, worry, or mental overload. Patients perceive that disconnect. Research has shown that emotional states are communicated energetically between people. Patients often sense whether a clinician is distracted, frustrated, rushed, or emotionally overwhelmed, even when those emotions are not verbally expressed.

How emotional regulation techniques can help

Clinical presence means being aligned in heart, mind, and emotions so that attention is fully available for the patient. It allows clinicians to listen deeply, communicate clearly, and respond with compassion and discernment.

When clinicians achieve this alignment, they become more present, connected, and effective. Patients often respond with greater trust, openness, and receptivity. In emergency medicine and high-pressure settings, this ability to quickly re-center can be especially critical.

HeartMath has worked extensively with EMTs, first responders, and frontline professionals because these professions must rapidly recover composure and clarity under intense pressure. HeartMath researched and created emotional regulation techniques that can be used in the moment to reset, realign, and regain access to higher cognitive functions in real time.

Coherence-building practices in clinical settings

One of the biggest misconceptions about emotional regulation is that it means suppressing emotions. Effective emotional regulation involves synchronizing breathing, heart rhythms, emotions, and mental focus so the autonomic nervous system becomes more balanced and coherent. HeartMath refers to these methods as coherence-building techniques because they strengthen synchronization between the heart, brain, and body.

The encouraging news for clinicians is that these practices do not require lengthy interventions. Many techniques can be practiced in one or two minutes between patients. The process is similar to rebooting a computer. A short reset can restore access to the system’s full capabilities.

Health professionals can use coherence practices to recover from emotional overload, improve resilience, and restore clinical presence during the workday. Over time, these techniques build resilience instead of building stress.

This moment-to-moment approach differs from traditional wellness advice that often focuses on recovery after work. Exercise, sleep, and self-care are all important, but clinicians also need tools they can use immediately during stressful situations and to release stress throughout the day.

Measuring and teaching emotional self-regulation

Emotional self-regulation skills are teachable and measurable in clinical environments. Heart rhythm biofeedback technologies such as the Inner Balance Coherence Plus sensor and the emWave Pro clinical system display the HRV pattern and autonomic nervous system interactions in real time. These tools show users when they are in coherence and when they are out of sync, and then train users to increase coherence in real time.

Because HRV patterns directly reflect emotional and physiological states, clinicians can use the heart rhythm biofeedback to train themselves to increase coherence and improve emotional regulation. Over time, they develop an internal reference for how coherence feels, making it easier to use a coherence technique in the midst of stressful moments even without biofeedback.

Studies have associated coherence-building techniques with reductions in medical errors, improvements in teamwork, and healthier workplace culture. Teams that practice coherence techniques often communicate more effectively and function with greater synchronization. Health professionals can also use these tools with patients, tracking progress and teaching practical regulation skills for anxiety, stress, and emotional overload.

Changing the culture of medicine

One of the greatest barriers to emotional regulation in medicine remains cultural. Many health care environments still reward chronic overwork and treat stress overload like a badge of honor, even as clinicians experience burnout, illness, and exhaustion. That culture is unsustainable and needs to change.

Health care professionals need tools to help them release stress while they work so they don’t accumulate it throughout the day. Emotional regulation and nervous system coherence are becoming essential because they help clinicians remain emotionally present, cognitively clear, and resilient, despite the complexity and uncertainty they face each day.

Medical schools, universities, and leadership programs are beginning to integrate these concepts and tools into their training, because they are recognizing that emotional coherence directly affects leadership capacity, patient care, and team effectiveness.

A hopeful direction for health care

Research on coherence practices continues to expand, with hundreds of published studies examining their effects on stress physiology, emotional states, and performance. Thousands of health professionals worldwide are already using coherence-building techniques and heart rhythm biofeedback tools in clinical practice with their patients and for themselves. They consistently report seeing improvements within a few weeks in emotional well-being, sleep, anxiety, and overall functioning.

As medicine increasingly recognizes the connection between nervous system regulation, emotional coherence, and cognitive performance, emotional regulation may become a defining competency for exceptional clinicians, and a foundation for effective care.

Deborah Rozman is a health care executive.

title: 张强医生：开发医疗AI须从医生日程表入手
summary: 医疗AI开发者需走进临床科室，理解各层级的医生效率痛点。...
full_content:
来源：HIT专家网      作者：周亦川

【编者按】

医疗AI热度高涨，各类产品层出不穷，一线临床医生的使用感受如何？HIT专家网推出“临床医生眼中的医疗AI”系列访谈，欢迎读者朋友关注。本期对话张强医生，分享他的医疗AI应用体会、思考与建议。

“医疗AI会在整体层面加速医疗的进展，尤其是放大医疗‘领头羊’的能力，一批原来艰难前行的优秀医生会借助AI的力量跑起来。”当前，医疗AI智能体快速发展迭代，在张强医生集团创始人、著名血管外科专家张强看来，最受益的是具有AI思维和驾驭能力的医生，AI对他们的赋能价值很可能远超其他人。

医疗AI初印象

2022年底，张强医生在一位患者家属强烈推荐之下安装了ChatGPT。不同于Dr.Watson一类医疗智能产品，与GPT简单交流几句，给他的第一印象是——机器终于能说“人话”了。

之后有几天，张强与这款产品反复对话、潜心研究，越来越觉得它“深不可测”：“它在临床决策、医学培训等多个方面给出了满意的规划。这使得我真的很崇拜它，甚至问它愿不愿意当医生集团的CEO？它居然没有回绝，并通过一整套令人信服的理由告诉我，医疗AI就是未来的大势所趋。”

张强用AI训练了一个虚拟助理（Virtual Assistant）并命名为Emily，在2023年7月集团九周年庆的培训中推荐给了员工。有趣的是，当时员工看到主讲人叫Emily，以为是外国讲者，直到演示时很多人才第一次了解到ChatGPT这一新的AI技术。这位虚拟助理不仅主导了整个会议的内容设计，还深度参与了讨论环节，员工与AI交互踊跃，解决了很多悬而未决的业务难题，大家也被医疗AI的效率与精准度深深折服。

AI正在重塑张强医生集团组织和效率

在张强医生集团，AI已从多个方面实现了节能增效的价值：

1.集团组织架构重构

AI智能体可以自主完成对话，因此张强医生集团撤销了客服部门，由AI负责与初诊患者对接。原先的客服部转为了智能部，员工每天交流训练智能体，减少AI幻觉；集团还撤销了品牌部，由AI负责患教内容与品牌宣传，仅保留少量人员做国际品牌输出。

2.战略决策智能辅助

张强说：“战略决策方面，AI起到了很大的作用。如果你只把它当作虚拟助理，它就会给你提供助理的答案；如果你把它任命为副总裁，它就会以最高管理者的视角考虑问题。”因此，张强任命Emily智能体为集团的“副总裁”，整合信息正是它的强项，再加上工作逻辑以及对未来的预判，不断为集团决策工作提供重要意见。

3.管理与决策模式重塑

张强医生集团取消了副总裁职位，转变为“人人拥有AI助理”的模式，员工角色从执行者转变为 AI 管理者。实践过程中，对Skill（即“能力单元”）理解应用越透彻的员工，越能够与其他人拉开工作效率的差距。

4.人机协作减少沟通成本

张强作为医生集团的管理者，过去每日大小琐事难以避免。自从人人有了AI助理，张强要求每位员工提交问题前都要先与AI交流，只有AI回答不好的问题再向上提交。结果显示，大多数的问题已经由AI消化，大大节约了双方时间。AI还充当了“翻译者”角色，消除了上下级及部门间的沟通壁垒。

在集团管理、患者服务方面，张强医生集团广泛铺开了AI应用，但在临床诊疗场景上，张强却很谨慎：“AI在提供优质信息的同时，也会带有无端编造的内容，也就是AI幻觉。它好比一把锋利的剑，对经验老到的高手是神兵利器，可以发现隐藏的肿瘤或是某些罕见病症的风险，有着深层次的应用潜力。但年资较浅的医生临床底蕴不牢，盲目挥舞，会被这柄剑伤到。一旦对AI产生依赖，没有仔细辨别AI提供信息的真伪，被编造的内容诱导，危害可能不是一点时间效率的损失，而是患者人身安全。” 

关于AI病历生成功能，张强也持比较保守的态度。在血管外科领域，AI自动生成的电子病历经医生审核修改，工作效率和准确性未较使用传统的病历模板有所提高。

从医生的日程表入手，开发实用的AI产品

究竟什么才是医生最需要的AI产品？

张强医生建议，IT人不应只拘泥于开发市场常见的产品功能，而是要走进医院，调研不同专业、不同资质的医生工作痛点。

比如，对于科主任而言，其工作场景有着更多的管理属性，日程表充斥着各种事务性工作，真正写病历或看影像片子用时并不多，具有多元化工作整理简化功能的AI产品就非常必要，林林总总、耗时几个星期的各种审批工作被压缩到一两天，即是最大的产品价值。

对于院领导而言，工作场景则更偏重于医院的战略问题，需大量时间和信息综合规划，如果信息整理不到位带来了方向偏差，几年的工作将付诸东流。在此场景下，AI应提供全面的行业信息整理和前瞻观察，致力于提供决策规划参考，而不只是一两项临床流程效率的提升。

由此可见，医疗AI的需求是分层、分场景的。IT人需充分理解AI究竟为谁服务。若开发产品时只是将医疗简单标签化，未能理解各个层级医生的诉求，未融入相应的工作场景，这往往是医生吐槽产品不实用的主要原因。

张强建议，AI产品开发过程中，IT人需认真分析每个用户的工作场景，一家医院的科主任和主治医师，以至大三甲医院与基层医院的医生工作需求也许完全不同。让AI真正能解决他们日程表上冗余的事务，使工作效率加倍，这才是最刚需的产品。

医疗AI对医生和医学教育的影响

张强对临床AI应用的几个热点话题给出了自己的思考意见：

1.AI会取代临床医生吗？

在张强看来，当前医生为每位患者付出的时间一再被压缩——门诊五分钟要看完一个病人，一天要连轴转十几台手术……医生已经不像医生，而是流水线上的“牛马”。医疗AI可以承担重复性、机械化的文书工作，将医生从流水线中解放出来，也可借助AI的力量总结经验、跟踪国际前沿进展，使其有更多时间进行临床思考、创新研究与患者人文关怀。因此，AI不一定会取代医生，而是通过效率整合，实现“让医生更像医生”的愿景。

另外，患者往往未必提供的是正确的信息，例如“很疼却说不疼”，“发生过某些事但说没有”。专业医生可以通过经验或追问，发现患者回答的矛盾之处加以修正，但AI只会全盘接收，推出的结果也就大相径庭了。无法辩明真伪，这也是AI难以取代医生的重要原因。

2.患者有病先问AI，是否会让实际诊疗沟通更困难？

张强认为，AI可以帮助患者解读化验单，降低了不必要的门诊量，当然也可能带来一定的误导，这就需要医生更有针对性地对病情进行解释。过去医生要为患者普及疾病基础知识，现在则是分析AI建议的合理性。

另外，AI可以为患者做术后患教管理，但也可能因为夸大或无视某些并发症症状，让患者过于紧张或是耽误了病情。功过相抵，AI的患者指导功能仍是一把“双刃剑”，医生需要在门诊诊疗和长期管理上做策略调整，适应医疗AI新潮流。

3.如何做好医疗AI时代的医学教育？

张强分析，我们常说医学生只会背书，学了几十本教科书但不会看病；进入临床之后，不少年轻医生的动手实力或许很强，但逻辑能力是硬伤。

“借助AI，过去一个学期的内容压缩成了两周，让医学生有更多的时间参与实践、创新探索，提高新时代下的综合诊疗能力。未来的医学教育应减少死记硬背的时间，取而代之的是重点培养软技能（Soft Skill），重点培养逻辑判断及驾驭AI的能力。”张强展望。

【责任编辑：陈曦 版式：明超 】

title: Universal health care after a calling became a job
summary: I think the battle for health care is lost. It started with HMOs in the 1980s and 1990s, which turned doctors into assembly-line workers. It got worse with the supposedly protective measure of work-ho...
full_content:
I think the battle for health care is lost.

It started with HMOs in the 1980s and 1990s, which turned doctors into assembly-line workers. It got worse with the supposedly protective measure of work-hour restrictions, further turning a “calling” into a “job.” That well-intended change had several unintended consequences. It shifted the applicant pool for medical school toward people who wanted to clock out at 5 p.m. A sense of responsibility to the patient, of seeing a problem through, was lost. The new doctors were also more inclined to follow employer mandates and less independent.

It continued with the electronic health record (EHR), which was never interoperable, so it failed the original stated goal of portable records. Institutions offset the exorbitant costs of these EHRs by turning doctors into billing and coding clerks, saving the salaries of the schedulers, billers, and coders they no longer had to staff. The unintended consequences were an erosion of the patient-physician relationship: introducing a device that sat between the patient and the physician, apparent distraction of the physician by data entry, loss of eye contact. Keeping up with the inbox and the records extended physician hours. Again, these changes degraded the calling of medicine and made it a less attractive profession.

It continues with prior authorization, where the medical professional is no longer in charge of making diagnostic or treatment decisions. Instead, they have to submit their plan to a for-profit insurance company that ultimately decides what is or is not allowed. It turns out that insurance companies are not even bound by these prior auths, as they rescind approval after the care is given.

The final blow is the almighty patient satisfaction score, which has replaced outcomes as the measure of a doctor. Doctors are now rated on scales just like nail salon technicians or barbers. Were they on time? Were they pleasant? Did they accommodate the customer’s requests for voodoo medicine instead of proven therapies?

The employer-insurance trap

There is one more chapter to this tragedy, and it sits on the patient side of the equation: the employer-provided health insurance “perk.” Most people do not realize that this benefit is really an often untaxed part of their salary, and the second-order effects are all harmful to patients. This perk for full-time employees leads many employers to hire a bunch of part-time workers instead of one full-time employee, to save on the cost of benefits. The employee representative who is negotiating with the insurer often has no clue what they are negotiating for, so critical goods and services get excluded to keep the cost of the insurance low. You do not know what has been negotiated away until you need it, like a bone marrow transplant for blood cancer.

Sometimes the employer opts for higher deductible and copay plans to control costs, again to the detriment of those insured. When deductibles and copays are high, people do not go in for health care. It is effectively like not having insurance. Sometimes the employer changes insurers, leaving employees with a policy that no provider accepts. This only enriches the substandard insurer by providing premiums that are never used. And many employees are afraid to leave an unsatisfying job for fear of losing this benefit, especially if they have a preexisting condition.

The solution, as always, is universal health care.

I am sure I missed some chapters in this tragedy. Maybe I will update this post as I remember them.

All I can say is, I am sure glad I am not at the start of my career. And I sure hope I can die without having to be a patient.

Banu Symington is a hematology-oncology physician.

title: 腾讯红杉连续投，在沙特开首家“没有医生”诊所的AI企业冲击IPO
summary: 6月30日，上海森亿智慧按18C特专科技规则申报港股主板IPO。其在沙特Almoosa医疗集团启动的AI诊所试点，为全球首个L4级AI诊所试点，不过海外试点尚未产生营收，企业基本盘仍在国内医院。森亿智慧自2016年成立，已完成9轮融资，累计超13亿元，股东包括腾讯、红杉等明星机构，投后估值约32亿，腾讯C、D、E三轮连投，红杉从Pre-A跟到E轮。选择森亿，一半因其团队，创始人张少典是哥伦比亚大学...
full_content:
6月30日，上海森亿智慧（前称为上海森亿医疗科技股份有限公司）再次向港股主板递交上市申请，按18C特专科技规则申报。


沙特Almoosa医疗集团启动试点运营的AI诊所内，一套由森亿智慧搭建的 AI 诊疗系统已经跑通全流程问诊，机器独立完成问诊、开出检查与用药方案，最后交由真人医生复核，这也是灼识咨询认证的全球首个L4级AI诊所试点。


招股书里写得很实在，这个海外试点目前只落地单家机构，还没产生实打实的营收，企业真正的基本盘依旧牢牢扎根国内各级医院。相对上次递表时提及的布局意向，这次已经完成了在沙特的落地。


有这样的能力自然会吸引资本的关注，翻开森亿股东名单，腾讯、红杉、IDG、GGV、真格、国药，这几乎是过去10年间中国医疗AI的全明星投资阵容。问题是AI 医疗发展了这么多年，商业化拐点真的来了吗？



先来看资本市场为什么集体押注这家公司。


森亿智慧自2016年成立至今，前后走完9轮融资，累计融资金额超13亿元，股东名单几乎集齐了国内最有话语权的产业巨头和美元基金。


创始人张少典+马汉东+员工持股平台合计控股约30%；外部方面，腾讯约13%，IDG关联方珠海慧亿约11%，红杉铭德约10%，GGV厦门纪源约5%。从2017年真格、树兰俊杰的天使轮，到2026年G轮天雅择亿、长发智胜进场，投后估值约32亿。


腾讯是C、D、E三轮连跟，红杉从Pre-A跟到E轮，IDG是E轮进场的。这种老股东反复加注+产业资本收尾的结构，在医疗AI里并不多见。


为什么选择森亿？一半看人，一半看赛道。


创始人张少典是哥伦比亚大学生物医学信息学博士，曾就职于IBM Watson，发表过31篇论文，被引用超过1900次，入选过《财富》40位40岁以下商界领袖。联合创始人马汉东发表过17篇研究论文、获10项专利；CTO殷嘉珩是全国首批一级AI认证人工智能训练师，主导开发了国内首个基于大语言模型的AI临床文书生成系统。


这不是一群IT人硬闯医院，而是懂医学的AI工程师在重新定义医疗信息化。


从赛道稀缺性来看，森亿走的是港股18C特专科技通道，面向未盈利的硬科技企业。


已上市的AI医疗企业，要么聚焦医疗大数据平台，要么单点突破影像AI。森亿的不同之处在于，它是国内仅有的实现L1到L4全栈商业化布局的企业，且按灼识咨询2025年的数据，在中国医院AI解决方案市场占5.1%，在大型医院（500张床位以上）AI解决方案市场占5.4%，两项都排名榜首。


森亿将智慧医疗发展定义为L1~L4阶段，图源招股书


财务数据也可以佐证。招股书显示，2023年~2025年，营收分别为2.39亿、2.92亿和3.25亿，毛利率为30.4%、37.1%和38.2%。经调整亏损从2.18亿到0.25亿，大幅收窄88.7%。


在AI医疗行业普遍烧钱换增长的背景下，亏损收窄说明公司的产品已经产生了真实的商业粘性，不是靠低价抢标得来的订单。灼识咨询预计中国智能医疗解决方案整体 2030 年近360亿，其中医院AI细分143亿，高阶L3赛道年增速63.5%。


长期成长空间摆在那儿，森亿又卡住了位置，资本没有不入场的理由。



拆解森亿四层产品布局，就能看懂公司业务的真实脉络。


森亿将自己的产品根据医院数据价值链搞了一套L1~L4的分级。L1是数据智能，帮医院把散落在各系统的数据梳理清楚，做运营分析和决策支持，这是基础。L2是AI辅助，像给医生配了个高级参谋，提供诊断建议和质控提醒，但决定权在人手里。L3是AI智能体，开始从参谋升级成同事，能主动参与临床路径、科研筛选和医院运营调度。L4就是沙特试点的样子，AI主导诊疗全流程，人类医生退到审核位。


企业4层产品布局，图源招股书


从它们各自的营收相比上次递表也出现了一些变化，L1产品毛利率34%，仍然占总收入一半以上；L2产品虽绝对值仍在增长（2025年1.55亿），但同比增速已从2024年的21%放缓至2025年的10%；L3虽因基数小而有1479%的爆发式增长，但也预示着市场需求的变化；L4则处于试点状态，还未产生收入，属于长期投资。


L1、L2的放缓和L3暴增的反差，恰好对应森亿正在经历的产品跃迁，即需求正在从传统的规则系统往Agent甚至自主诊疗开始跃迁。


这一切离不开森亿自研的Synapse技术底座，它把数据治理和模型训练框架打包在一起，L1到L4共用同一套技术内核，越往后做边际成本越低，所以L3的毛利率能做到54%、在L2放缓时L3能递补。


客户数据也能侧面证明，相对上次递表的数据，森亿目前覆盖了超过800多家医院，其中包括400多家大型三甲医院，900家终端客户（上次仅750家医院）。客户三年收入留存率达112.7%，老客户不仅没流失，反而持续在增购更多产品。


在医疗信息化行业，客户换供应商的成本极高，能做到留存率超100%，说明森亿已经从卖软件变成了建平台。再加上投标中标率逐年走高，三甲标杆案例的口碑效应正在形成。



据灼识咨询2025年的数据，尽管森亿在中国医院AI解决方案市场和大型医院AI解决方案市场均排榜首，但市占率均在5%左右，市场还很分散，未来有巨大的整合空间。


为什么如此分散？政策端其实已经催了好几年。包括智慧医院评级要求EMR达到5级、互联互通四甲，卫健委明确提出2027年70%的三级医院要落地AI应用，2024年11月AI辅助诊断正式纳入医保，再加上DRG/DIP支付改革，这些措施都在倒逼医院降本增效，不上智能化，医院控成本的工具箱就会少一件。


招股书里的数据也显示，中国智能医疗解决方案市场预计将从2025年的180亿元增长至2030年的359亿元，其中高阶产品市场年增速63.5%。背后的驱动力主要来自政策端的加码。


可行业痛点也摆在那里，医院的HIS、EMR系统五花八门，数据碎片化容易形成信息孤岛；市面上的通用大模型缺少临床指南支撑，落地容易合规难；市面上很多单点工具，影像AI、病历AI、管理AI各自为政，没有全套解决方案。


森亿的生态位恰好卡在这些痛点的交汇处，Synapse底座解决数据孤岛问题，百万级医学知识图谱解决大模型合规问题，L1-L4的全栈产品解决产品割裂问题。


募资用途中除了持续产品研发迭代外，最值得关注的是海外市场的拓展。森亿准备搭建中东、东南亚本地化销售与交付中心，招聘熟悉沙特、阿联酋医疗政策的本地员工，参加海外医疗行业峰会，复制 AI 诊所落地模式。


目前，沙特试点仅单机构，缺乏本地销售运营团队，募资搭建海外渠道才能批量对接中东医疗机构的需求。特别是2025年~2030年，中东智能医疗解决方案市场复合增长率达26.6%，远高于全球的15.6%和国内14.8%。


国内医院项目普遍回款周期在180天~270天，是医疗信息化行业的共性难题，也是企业布局海外对冲国内周期的核心原因。


张少典回国创业已经是第九年，森亿也走到了港交所的门口。腾讯、红杉、IDG等明星资本参与了9轮、投入13个亿，押的是国内三甲医院智能化改造的市场和海外的增量。资本期待中国医院AI第一股的兑现。

title: 首发 | 超6亿美元！英矽智能再次拿下AI制药大订单
summary: 2026年7月2日，由生成式人工智能（AI）驱动的临床阶段药物研发科技公司英矽智能宣布，已与武田制药（Takeda）达成全球战略合作协议，双方将依托英矽智能自主研发的一体化端到端Pharma.AI平台，在武田制药选定多个疾病治疗领域共同推进候选药物研发。此次合作旨在针对具备高潜力的治疗靶点，发现具有临床差异化优势的候选药物。通过将先进的生成式AI模型应用于早期药物研发阶段，双方团队有望全面提升设计...
full_content:
动脉网第一时间获悉，7月2日，由生成式人工智能（AI）驱动的临床阶段药物研发科技公司英矽智能（Insilico Medicine，HKEX：3696）宣布，已与武田制药（Takeda）达成全球战略合作协议，双方将依托英矽智能自主研发的一体化端到端 Pharma.AI平台，在武田制药选定多个疾病治疗领域共同推进候选药物研发。


此次合作旨在针对具备高潜力的治疗靶点，发现具有临床差异化优势的候选药物。通过将先进的生成式AI模型应用于早期药物研发阶段，双方团队有望全面提升设计候选分子的质量，并进行结构优化，以期达到同类最佳（Best-in-class）的疗效及理想的安全性标准。


根据协议，英矽智能将主导AI驱动的药物发现工作，负责设计并筛选出符合预设科学标准及早期开发标准的候选分子；武田制药则将发挥其强大的全球开发能力，推进相关候选药物的临床验证。 英矽智能将获得约6000万美元的项目启动费和近期里程碑付款；此外，还有资格获得基于临床前、临床、商业化及销售进展的里程碑付款，总金额最高可达约6亿美元；以及产品商业化后基于未来净销售额的分级特许权使用费。武田制药将获得此次合作筛选出的新型疗法在全球范围内的独家开发、生产和商业化权利。

 

英矽智能创始人、首席执行官兼首席商务官 Alex Zhavoronkov 博士 表示：“我们非常荣幸能与全球生物制药巨头武田制药开展深度合作。武田在拥抱生成式AI方面展现出了远见与实力。随着我们将生成式AI深化应用至制药价值链的各个环节，我相信‘制药超级智能’的未来将为全球患者带来质量更高、差异化更大的药物。这也是我们践行‘延长人类健康并提高生产力寿命’的重要一步。 ”

 

武田制药首席科学官兼研发负责人 Chris Arendt 博士表示：“此次合作将武田在疾病生物学领域的深厚专业积累，与英矽智能前沿的AI药物发现能力完美结合，致力于通过识别具备临床差异化优势的疗法，为患者带来更有意义的治疗选择。这也标志着武田向‘AI原生（AI）’药物发现模式转型的又一里程碑。我们正通过整合自动化、机器人技术和生成式AI，更高效地推进高质量候选药物的研发管线。”

 

英矽智能是一家全球先锋生物科技公司，致力于整合人工智能和自动化技术，加速药物发现并推动生命科学领域的创新，赋能人类更长久更健康的生活。2025年12月30日，公司于香港联交所主板挂牌上市，股票代码：03696.HK。

 

利用自主研发的Pharma.AI 平台和先进的自动化生物学实验室，英矽智能正在为纤维化、肿瘤学、免疫学、疼痛、肥胖和代谢紊乱等未满足的疾病领域提供创新药物解决方案。此外，英矽智能持续将Pharma.AI应用拓展到多元化领域，如先进材料、农业、营养产品及兽医药物。

 

武田制药以"为人类创造健康生活，为世界缔造美好未来"为使命。我们专注于消化和炎症性疾病、罕见病、血液制品、肿瘤、神经科学及疫苗等关键治疗领域，着力研发并为患者带来突破性的创新疗法。我们的目标是携手合作伙伴，通过我们动态且多样化的产品管线，不断改善患者体验，并拓展对前沿性治疗方案的探索。武田制药总部位于日本，作为一家以价值观为基础、以研发为驱动的全球化生物制药公司，我们始终致力于兑现对患者、员工和地球地承诺。我们遍布全球80多个国家和地区的员工肩负着相同的使命，始终践行着两个多世纪以来形成的价值观。

title: When local doctors disappear, patients pay the price [PODCAST]
summary: Subscribe to The Podcast by KevinMD. Watch on YouTube. Catch up on old episodes! A patient with a dying leg won’t drive 20 miles for care. In Devin Zarkowsky’s town, that’s not a hyp...
full_content:
Subscribe to The Podcast by KevinMD. Watch on YouTube. Catch up on old episodes!

A patient with a dying leg won’t drive 20 miles for care. In Devin Zarkowsky’s town, that’s not a hypothetical. Vascular surgeon Devin Zarkowsky runs a solo office-based practice in a San Diego County town where peripheral arterial disease patients had nowhere local to go, and Jason McKitrick is the executive director of the Office-Based Facility Association. They discuss the KevinMD article “Why local care matters for peripheral arterial disease.” You will hear how the Medicare physician fee schedule pays hospitals for big-ticket equipment but pays solo doctors out of the same bundled rate they use to cover staff and wire and drapes, why a four-figure atherectomy or Shockwave catheter swallows a week of revenue when there is no separate reimbursement, and why Congress is finally looking at fixing it after years of small fixes that did not close the gap. You will hear what local care actually means when a patient cannot drive and the limb cannot wait.

Partner with me on the KevinMD platform. With over three million monthly readers and half a million social media followers, I give you direct access to the doctors and patients who matter most. Whether you need a sponsored article, email campaign, video interview, or a spot right here on the podcast, I offer the trusted space your brand deserves to be heard. Let’s work together to tell your story.

PARTNER WITH KEVINMD → https://kevinmd.com/influencer

SUBSCRIBE TO THE PODCAST → https://www.kevinmd.com/podcast

RECOMMENDED BY KEVINMD → https://www.kevinmd.com/recommended

Transcript

Kevin Pho: Hi, and welcome to the show. Subscribe at kevinmd.com/podcast. Today, welcome Devin Zarkowsky and Jason McKitrick. Devin is a vascular surgeon. Jason is the executive director for the Office-Based Facility Association. The KevinMD article we’re talking about today is “Why local care matters for peripheral arterial disease.”

Devin and Jason, welcome to the show.

Devin Zarkowsky: Thank you very much, Kevin. We sincerely appreciate it.

Jason McKitrick: Thank you very much.

Kevin Pho: All right. So I’m just going to ask both of you to briefly introduce yourselves, and then we’ll kick off with the article. Devin, why don’t you go first?

Devin Zarkowsky: Well, thank you, Kevin, for having us here. We appreciate the opportunity to talk on this podcast.

I am a vascular surgeon. I have a solo practice office-based facility in Fallbrook, California. I’ve been in Southern California for several years now, and there’s a lot of underserved peripheral arterial disease patients that we’re hoping to take care of.

Kevin Pho: All right. And Jason, you’ve been on the show before, but for those who forgot who you were, just give us a little reminder and briefly share your story.

Jason McKitrick: Jason McKitrick, Liberty Partners Group principal and the executive director of the Office-Based Facility Association.

Kevin Pho: All right. So the article we’re talking about is “Why local care matters for peripheral arterial disease.” Devin, for those who didn’t get a chance to read this article, just share why you decided to write it, and then about the article itself for those that didn’t get a chance to read it yet.

Devin Zarkowsky: Yeah, absolutely. I think over the course of my training through medical school, residency, fellowship, I’ve been an academic, I’ve been a private practice doctor, but I did a lot of work early on looking at barriers to care, disparities in care. I had a particularly important mentor of mine, Philip Goodney at Dartmouth, who had a map in a 2013 article showing areas that, you know, were underserved and had a lot of amputations associated with peripheral arterial disease and diabetes.

It happens that I landed in an area of the world where that is occurring. Something I learned in residency was that folks tend not to want to leave too far from where they live to go seek care, and that’s a pattern in Southern California that holds true. I have people in my town that don’t want to drive more than 15, 20 minutes even though they might have a black toe, and that’s happened again and again.

As I’ve decided where to lay roots, it’s been in an area of San Diego County that just is unusually underserved. And so that’s what brings us here today to discuss how best to bring care to underserved environments.

Kevin Pho: So Devin, just to follow up, you mentioned that you have an individual private practice. So as a vascular surgeon, how common is that in the United States for vascular surgeons to have solo practices like the one you have?

Devin Zarkowsky: Phenomenal question. I want it to be more common. I tell my friends all the time, you know, this style of practice really brings you back to your roots. It’s you and the patient talking about medicine.

The classic vascular surgeon over the last 30 years has been a hospital-based doctor. You know, the surgeries that we do for open care, they’re complex. These patients are very sick. They require a hospital setting if you make an incision. What’s interesting about the last 20, 25 years of the endovascular revolution is technology has really made it possible for us to do this in an office-based setting under local anesthetic.

And so all of that complexity falls away when you don’t have the physiologic dyscrasia associated with open surgery. And so it is becoming more and more common for vascular specialists to practice in the manner that I practice.

Kevin Pho: So Jason, you are the policy expert kind of bringing that perspective to this discussion. Tell us about some of the threats from a policy standpoint that solo practitioners like Dr. Zarkowsky are facing today.

Jason McKitrick: Well, one of the reasons I’m grateful to be back on your show, Dr. Kevin, is that you may recall previously we had an interventional radiologist on. But there are many kinds of office-based specialists. IRs in the case of Dr. Zarkowsky, vascular surgeons, interventional cardiologists. And even going outside of endovascular, radiation oncology, urology, many, many kinds of specialists that are similarly situated to Dr. Zarkowsky.

So what we’re seeing in the office-based setting, which is really not well understood relative to what happens in a hospital or an ASC, in the office-based setting you can do a lot of these really advanced technologies minimally invasively with, as Dr. Zarkowsky said, you know, moderate sedation. And do it as a lowest cost option. But because of the really significant hospital health system consolidation over the years, many of those options are going away as an option, particularly in rural areas. So that’s a big threat and something we’re working hard to reverse.

Kevin Pho: So Jason, tell us specifically about the policy threats that we’re talking about that make practices like Dr. Zarkowsky’s very difficult to find and spread.

Jason McKitrick: Well, you know, there’s been a lot of talk in recent years about what’s called the Medicare physician fee schedule and how badly it needs to be reformed.

And what has happened over the years is as these high-tech but higher cost, you know, supplies and equipment have come out of the hospitals to allow guys like Dr. Zarkowsky to do their job, the physician fee schedule, Medicare physician fee schedule, there’s been a lot of discussion about reforming it in recent years. And the reason that, the fundamental reason why it needs reform is that as high-tech equipment and supplies have come out of the hospital and gone into the office-based setting to allow guys like Dr. Zarkowsky to do their job, the money from a reimbursement standpoint has not gone with it.

And so the physician fee schedule effectively has been subsidizing the hospital setting for years. And so we think if that’s the problem, then the answer is to fix that, and to pull those practice expenses out of the physician fee schedule and pay for those practice expenses in the same kind of way that we pay for practice expense in the hospital. That would go a long way to stabilizing office-based care and a long way towards parity between those two settings.

Kevin Pho: So Devin, as you run your solo practice, just tell us what it’s like on the ground about some of these policy implications that Jason’s describing. How does that affect you individually? Maybe give us some stories about how those policy changes affect you on an individual level.

Devin Zarkowsky: Great segue because I was just going to say I wrote a check this morning for about one-seventh of my bank account that I have to run my practice to support equipment costs. And so I think what Mr. McKitrick is getting at is the necessary expenditures that are associated with properly equipping a safe outpatient office. And I take this seriously. You know, I want to make sure that when a person comes into my office for care, that the experience is going to be equivalent to or better than ambulatory surgery center, hospital setting that they might be exposed to otherwise, and that comes with a significant cost.

And so when Mr. McKitrick talks about stabilizing the practice fee structure for physicians, this is exactly what he’s getting at. I would, you know, I’m always looking at how to make endovascular care better. And to be able to have equal footing at least with, you know, hospital systems and ambulatory surgery centers in the area would be a boom.

I’ll give you an example. When we have somebody with recalcitrant calcium, and I’m thinking about a case from earlier this week, we have wire, sheath, drapes, and staff which are par for the course. Those are not necessarily the big-ticket items. But to really get a good result, there’s atherectomy catheter, and now thankfully at the turn of the year, we have Shockwave, which is an intravascular lithotripsy device. And both of those devices are four-figure expenditures that are reimbursed as part of the RVU structure from Medicare. If there was a way that we could segment them out similar to the way the hospitals and ASCs do, you know, I’m thinking about this case earlier this week, it would’ve made a significant difference in how I’m able to run my practice and staff it.

And so I think ultimately what we’re getting at is restoring the medical fabric in this particular town, Fallbrook, which has been affected by all of the consolidation in the area. You know, the patients that I take care of come to me for this sort of procedure because they have to drive, you know, 15, 20 miles in either direction. And I’m glad to be there, and I hope to be there for years and years to come and reestablish the medical fabric in that community.

Kevin Pho: Devin, you wrote about a patient’s story in your article. So if you weren’t around to provide that local care, just give us a sense of how removal of that option would affect patients. If you weren’t available, where would patients go for vascular care?

Devin Zarkowsky: So I had a patient last week who had bone fall out of a wound in their foot in my office. And so the answer in many cases is they won’t. The savvy consumer of health care, which unfortunately a lot of my patient population is not, would drive. There’s a 20-mile drive north, a 20-mile drive south that they could make. But in my particular patient population, that is challenging because the limb is dying, and it’s hard to get around if you can’t walk, let alone drive.

And so when we talk about the heat map, like Dr. Goodney drew in 2013, the issue is multifaceted. It is being a good consumer, it is having the resources, it is being mobile to get to the place, and then having the wherewithal to work within a system that is complicated to get the care that you need. By being in the town that I’m in, I provide that resource without much effort. Does that answer your question? It’s a long-winded answer, I apologize.

Kevin Pho: No, absolutely. I’m interested in hearing, of course, what we’re talking about as to how it relates to patients, because really, that’s the core issue, and that’s what it comes down to, of course. Jason, from a policy standpoint, are we making any headway? Because I know that we’ve had a similar conversation a few months ago. Anything on the horizon from the policy standpoints? Do our policymakers and legislatures, is this on their radar? Do you expect some movement regarding this going forward?

Jason McKitrick: There actually is, I’m glad to say. I mean, the 2026 physician fee schedule reversed some of the bad news. We talked on a previous podcast how in the physician fee schedule in 2025 there were 300 office-based interventional services where the reimbursement was literally less than the cost to do them. The 2026 physician fee schedule that started January 1 of this year helped to reverse some of that.

But Congress does actually, it looks like they may try to do something much more fundamental going forward. There was a hearing, a couple hearings in the Congress this past week, one with Ways and Means and one with Energy and Commerce Committee. And in the Energy and Commerce Committee, they actually talked about the very issue we’re talking about, that the practice expense portion of the physician fee schedule is flawed. And one of the members, Congressman Joyce, talked about this very concept about paying for practice expense in the office-based setting more like we pay for practice expense in the hospital-based setting.

So everything we’ve been talking about, the Congress has actually really taken a hard look at, and we may just very well see some movement here later this year, which would be amazing.

Kevin Pho: We’re talking to Devin Zarkowsky and Jason McKitrick. Devin is a vascular surgeon. Jason is the executive director for the Office-Based Facility Association. The KevinMD article we’re talking about today is “Why local care matters for peripheral arterial disease.” Now I’m going to ask each of you just to end with some take-home messages that you want to share with the KevinMD audience. Jason, I’m going to have you go first, and then we’ll end with Devin.

Jason McKitrick: Well, I think the take-home message here is that after many, many years of talking about reform to the physician fee schedule and, more broadly, about these concerns about hospital consolidation and what that means for patient cost shares and patient access, particularly in rural areas, it looks like Congress may very well try to tackle those issues, which would be gratifying and ultimately, as you said, Dr. Devin, would really be great for the patients. So, fingers crossed.

Kevin Pho: And Devin, before you share your take-home message, just explain to us why you still do what you do despite the financial headwinds. I’m sure it would be easier financially for you to join a bigger group in a bigger city. So tell us why you do what you do and then end off with your take-home messages.

Devin Zarkowsky: My father was a solo practice dentist for 40 years in a small town. This is unusual, but I thought I was going to be an academic ivory tower kind of guy. It just didn’t turn out to be me. I’m slowly turning into my father, quite frankly, and it’s the nature versus nurture argument there. I’ve just found that I’m happier on my own out in this corner of the world, you know, plowing a field. It’s just a me thing. It’s not really necessarily everyone. So, that’s what I would say.

You know, takeaway here, physicians, we need to unite. We need to find our common voice. These communities that we work in, there is a fabric that we need to restore by rediscovering the root of medicine, which is the doctor and patient relationship. I mean, this is not scalable. AI will never supplant it. We can’t listen to people telling us that we’re not good at business. I mean, we memorize the various renal tubular acidoses and what the Edinger-Westphal complex does, so we can use AI to create a balance sheet and a P&L statement for goodness sakes.

You know, the route forward is through taking control of our future as a profession, and stabilizing practice payments in favor of outpatient treatment is the way to do it. You know, I think Mr. McKitrick is exactly right on that point.

Kevin Pho: Devin and Jason, thank you so much for sharing your stories, time, and insight. Thanks again for coming on the show.

Devin Zarkowsky: Thank you very much, Dr. Pho. Appreciate it.

title: 终于又有NGS企业准备IPO了
summary: 2026年6月底，南京世和基因向科创板提交招股申请书，近两年肿瘤NGS企业冲刺IPO消息罕见。此前，燃石、泛生子登陆纳斯达克后表现不佳，中国资本市场也冷清，臻和科技三次递表港交所失效，吉凯基因等在科创板折戟，赛道沉寂三年。2015 - 2020年，肿瘤NGS发展迎第一波浪潮，资本涌入，LDT模式盛行；2020 - 2025年，LDT模式天花板显现，燃石、泛生子在美股上市后不被资本市场看好。2025...
full_content:
2026年6月底，南京世和基因再次向科创板提交招股申请书。


近两年的二级市场，很难看见肿瘤NGS企业冲刺IPO的消息。2020年燃石、泛生子扎堆登陆纳斯达克，一度推高行业热度，可短短几年时间，燃石股价脚踝斩，泛生子更是直接走完私有化流程。


中国资本市场同样冷清，臻和科技三次递表港交所，三次失效。吉凯基因、联川生物等一批排队者，也在科创板门口折戟。赛道进入长达三年的沉寂期。


一时间，投资人谈NGS色变，从业者谈上市摇头。


时隔两年多，世和基因换了新的申报稿，再度冲击科创板。


沉寂这么久，为什么是世和基因先回来？这家企业手里的故事、业绩、合规底牌，和上一次相比到底发生了哪些变化？这一份重新递交的招股书，或许藏着整个肿瘤NGS行业的答案。



要理解世和基因为什么再次提交招股书，得先看这个行业经历了什么。


2015年~2020年，是肿瘤NGS发展的第一波浪潮。资本持续涌入，LDT（临床实验室自建项目）模式大行其道。企业不需要拿注册证，只要在实验室里把检测跑通，就依靠渠道向患者端推广。那时候，讲的故事是精准医疗和蓝海市场。


2020年~2025年开始退潮，LDT模式的天花板很快显现，由于没有统一标准，进不了医保，医院端的合规风险越来越大。燃石和泛生子在美股上市时，讲的是中国大市场肿瘤NGS检测服务商的故事，但资本市场认为没有产品化能力的服务商，本质上是一家人力密集型的医学检验所。


真正的拐点出现在2025年底，国家医保局首次把高通量基因测序纳入全国统一病理收费体系，结束各地定价混乱的局面，为 NGS 大范围进医保铺平底层制度道路，同时也开始简化NGS试剂盒审评流程。


海南乐城先行区放开境外获批试剂进口通道也为行业打开了新的增长窗口。世和基因抓住机会在2025年12月获批设立海南世和宁鳌医学检验实验室，于2026年1月获准进口其已获美国FDA上市许可的泛实体瘤大Panel基因检测试剂盒作为临床急需进口医疗器械，可面向全国接收样本。


行业走完了喧嚣、沉寂、拐点的三个阶段。现在比拼的不是检测量，而是谁家的试剂盒能率先拿证、进医保。


横向看国内企业的产品进度，已经开始分层。燃石和泛生子经历波折后，重心开始转向美国市场拿证，国内管线推进相对放缓。臻和科技至今没有大Panel试剂盒获批，三次闯关港股失效后，产品落地的窗口期正在收窄。吉因加等企业仍在临床验证阶段。艾德生物虽然上市且盈利，但主力是PCR小Panel，NGS大Panel并非其核心战场，两家其实不在同一个细分赛道里。


世和基因是目前国内少数手握大Panel+小Panel+泛实体瘤伴随诊断多款获批试剂盒，且在大Panel上实现中国、美国和欧盟三地拿证的企业，液体活检与泛癌早筛管线也已分别进入创新审查与FDA突破性认定阶段。



产品矩阵最直观的反馈是企业营收结构。


2023年，世和基因的临床检测服务还占营收的58.43%，检测产品销售只占22.62%。典型的LDT服务商模式。到了2025年，占比倒过来了，检测产品销售营收2.67亿元占比46.6%，临床检测服务收入2.25亿元，占比降到39.4%。试剂盒正式成为第一增长曲线。


这个反转是靠四张注册证在支撑。


2018年，国内首批六基因小Panel试剂盒，覆盖肺癌基础靶向检测。


2023年10月，国内首个大Panel试剂盒世和一号获批，用于非小细胞肺癌组织TMB检测，填补国产大Panel空白。


2025年8月，泛实体瘤大Panel产品GENESEEQPRIME拿到FDA上市许可，是全球仅有的同时获得中国、美国、欧盟三重认证的高通量大Panel肿瘤检测产品。


2025年10月，NTRK基因融合检测试剂盒获批，解决罕见肿瘤靶点检测需求，成为国内首个基于NGS的泛实体瘤NTRK伴随诊断。


四张证，把世和基因从服务商转向为产品公司。


此外，2024年，用于检测患者外周血中肿瘤游离ctDNA的EGFR/KRAS/MET基因突变检测试剂盒成功进入创新医疗器械特别审查程序，为国内高通量测序多基因液体活检领域首创。


企业产品布局，图源招股书


产品顺利推进离不开学术背书，主打多癌筛查的世和鹰眼CanScan拿到美国FDA突破性医疗器械认定；金陵队列超万人前瞻性临床研究成果刊发在《Nature Medicine》；AI辅助基因变异解析模块在2026年被纳入FDA人工智能医疗器械清单。这些都是企业产品的护城河。


只是客观来看，世和现阶段营收体量算不上行业龙头，2025年全年营收5.77亿元，仍有提升空间。毕竟燃石经历发展过山车后营收也与世和相当，艾德生物2025年营收规模也在世和之上。


世和基因现在的位置更像是一个已经完成检测服务商向IVD器械企业转型，有一定技术护城河、但规模中等的突围者。



资本层面的故事，比技术更耐人寻味。


世和创始团队是典型科研底色组合，董事长邵阳为多伦多大学癌症生物学博士，父亲邵华武是中科院药物化学博士，曾任加拿大国家研究院研究员。妻子汪笑男也是多伦多大学细胞与分子生物学硕士。创始团队是一套典型的技术+学院双背书的家族创业结构。


这种背景在科创板很吃香。但科创板认的不只是学历，还有估值的合理性。


世和基因的融资历程堪称跌宕。2019年D轮融资时，投前估值冲到55亿元。2024年4月撤回IPO后，部分机构股东的回购条款触发，实控人一度面临回购履约压力。之后，邵阳、汪笑男、赵忞超设立SPV平台普络格和费纳力，归集老股，同时引入新投资者。


2025年底，平安系旗下和谐安瑞等产业资本进场。


平安系愿意在低谷期接手，看的不是肿瘤NGS这个概念，而是具体的产品线。大Panel已经在国内获批，FDA证已经拿到，NTRK试剂盒已经上市，海南博鳌乐城的IVD化通道已经打通。这些是看得见摸得着的资产，不是PPT里的故事。


据招股书披露，本次IPO拟募资17亿元，近六成的资金投入早筛与MRD试剂盒研发，6.6亿元砸向金陵队列与泛癌早筛试剂盒，1.8亿元做MRD（术后微小残留病灶检测）试剂盒，1.3亿元布局肿瘤AI大模型。其AI系统已通过FDA相关审核，可辅助测序数据智能解读，是贯穿全产品线的底层技术。剩余资金用于渠道铺设和补充流动资金，符合行业长期高研发投入的特性。


结合企业产品来看，早筛和MRD确实是肿瘤NGS的下一波增量，只是早筛这条线，从临床验证到获批上市再到进医保，商业化周期通常需要5年~8年，乐观估计也要到2030年前后才会形成稳定收入。


短期业绩真正的看点，还是大Panel产品在国内医院的放量，以及海外市场的渗透。AI模块虽然进了FDA清单，但在肿瘤诊疗领域，AI目前更多是效率工具，不是收费主体。


募资资金分配和行业增量赛道虽然匹配，但回报周期也存在明确时间差，需要长期观察。


说到底，NGS行业不是不行了，它只是从用服务讲故事的阶段，进展到产品拼注册的阶段。


2020年那波热潮，燃石和泛生子向全球资本市场证明了中国肿瘤NGS的需求真实存在；这次世和基因二次冲击科创板，可能要证明的是，中国企业能把技术变成标准化、可复制的医疗产品。
